blocks.text: Chapter 2.
Skewness: Why Being Right Isn't the Point
The ideas in 30 seconds

What matters in any bet is expectation — probability multiplied by the size of the outcome — not how often you turn out to be right; you can be wrong 90% of the time and still get rich if your rare wins dwarf your frequent losses.

Under skewed (asymmetric) payoff distributions, a track record is nearly worthless: a strategy can look brilliant for years while quietly carrying a rare, catastrophic loss that simply hasn't shown up in the sample yet.

Traders face a structural choice between bleed (small frequent losses, rare huge wins) and blowup (small frequent wins, rare ruin); markets and careers systematically reward the blowup profile right up until it detonates.

Bull-market stars like Carlos and John embodied survival of the least fit: the exact traits that made them money in one regime — stubbornness, averaging down, marrying positions — destroyed them when the regime flipped.

Stephen Jay Gould's cancer lesson — the median isn't the message — shows why averages and medians mislead whenever a distribution is asymmetric: the typical outcome and the expected outcome can point in opposite directions.
Expectation, Not Frequency: The Bullish-but-Short Meeting

At a weekly trading meeting, Taleb was asked whether he thought the market would go up. He said it was quite likely to rise over the coming week — he put roughly a 70% probability on it. A colleague then pointed out, triumphantly, that Taleb was heavily short S&P 500 futures. Contradiction? Not at all.

Taleb believed the market would probably drift up a little, but that if it fell, it could fall violently. Sketch the arithmetic: a 70% chance of gaining 1% contributes +0.7% to the expectation; a 30% chance of losing 10% contributes −3%. The expected value — each outcome weighted by its probability — is decisively negative even though "up" is the most likely single outcome. So the rational position is short.

The room couldn't process this because most people, including most professionals, conflate probability (how often something happens) with expectation (probability times magnitude). Financial commentary makes the confusion worse: analysts are graded on whether their calls were "right," a pure frequency measure that ignores payoff size entirely. A forecaster who is right 95% of the time about small moves and catastrophically wrong 5% of the time is a menace, not a sage.

This is why Taleb dismisses the question "do you think the market is going up or down?" as journalism, not trading. In symmetric games — a coin flip for even money — frequency and expectation coincide, and intuition works fine. The moment payoffs become asymmetric, frequency of correctness becomes irrelevant and can be actively misleading. Being right is not the point; making money when you're right, and losing little when you're wrong, is the entire point.
Skewness: Why Rare Events Hide From Track Records

A distribution is skewed when its outcomes are lopsided — many small results on one side, a few enormous ones on the other. Under skewness, the past is a treacherous guide, because the event that defines the strategy's true economics may not have occurred yet within your observation window.

Consider a strategy that earns a steady $1 a month by, in effect, selling insurance against a rare disaster. For years the track record shows smooth, consistent profits, low volatility, a gorgeous Sharpe ratio. The disaster strikes once a decade and costs $500. Anyone evaluating the manager on a five-year record is measuring noise: the single number that determines whether the strategy is profitable at all — the size and frequency of the rare loss — is invisible in the sample.

Taleb calls this the rare-event problem: rare events cannot be estimated from empirical frequency precisely because they are rare. Worse, the asymmetry is usually adverse. Strategies that "look good" in short samples are disproportionately the ones that harvest small premiums by silently shorting rare events, since that is exactly what produces smooth returns. The smoother and more consistent a return stream looks, the more suspicious you should be about what's lurking in the unobserved tail.

The statistical vocabulary matters here. Variance treats upside and downside symmetrically, so it fails as a risk measure under skewness — a strategy can have tiny measured volatility and unlimited hidden downside. Realized past volatility understates true risk whenever the loss-making event hasn't happened yet in the sample. This also explains why markets can misprice rare events for long stretches: participants who bet against them get stopped out, fired, or ridiculed long before they're vindicated. History books eventually record the rare event; monthly performance reviews never do.
Bleed vs Blowup: Taleb's Out-of-the-Money Options Practice

Every asymmetric strategy sits on one side of a trade-off. Blowup traders collect small, frequent profits and carry a rare, ruinous loss: they sell options, sell volatility, sell insurance against the improbable. Bleed traders accept small, frequent losses in exchange for rare, enormous gains: they buy that same insurance. Mathematically, either side can have positive expectation; psychologically and institutionally, the two lives could not be more different.

Taleb's own practice was to buy out-of-the-money options — cheap contracts that pay nothing in normal times but pay off explosively when the market makes a violent move. This positions him long the rare event: capped, known downside (the small premiums), open-ended upside. The cost is the bleed — losing a little money almost every day, sometimes for years, while waiting for a dislocation.

He is candid that the bleed is agonizing precisely because of frequency. A bleed trader is wrong most days, most weeks, most quarters. Colleagues snicker; management asks pointed questions; self-doubt compounds, because humans experience the pain of losses roughly twice as intensely as the pleasure of equivalent gains, and frequent small losses deliver that pain on a drip feed. The blowup trader, by contrast, enjoys daily reinforcement, a growing bonus, and a swelling reputation — until one morning erases everything, often including the firm's capital, which is a hidden negative that never appeared in his P&L.

Institutions amplify the bias. Bonuses are annual and never clawed back, so a trader who blows up in year seven keeps six years of bonuses; the payoff structure of employment itself is short the rare event. Taleb's conclusion: given human wiring and incentive design, the market chronically overpopulates the blowup side, which is exactly why the patient bleed side can be profitable — if you can stand it.
Survival of the Least Fit: Carlos and John

Carlos was an elegant, cultivated emerging-markets bond trader — polished economist, impeccable English, patrician manners. From 1992 through mid-1998 he made money almost every year, and his signature move was buying the dips: whenever emerging-market debt sold off, he added to his position, and the market always came back. His employers credited his economics; in truth, the market regime credited him. A rising, mean-reverting market makes dip-buyers look like geniuses because their defining trait — refusing to fold — is temporarily optimal.

Then came the summer of 1998 and Russia's default. Bonds collapsed; Carlos averaged down; they collapsed further; he averaged down again, insisting the market was "irrational" and his positions represented value. By the time he was fired, he had lost more than the cumulative total of everything he had earned in his career. John, the high-yield trader whose 1998 wipeout Chapter 1 describes, followed the same script with leverage: years of $250 million profits, then roughly $600 million gone in days.

Taleb's diagnosis is survival of the least fit. These men were not skilled traders enjoying bad luck; they were the lucky products of a specific random cycle, selected by that cycle precisely for traits that guarantee eventual destruction. He catalogs the shared markers: overestimating the precision of their beliefs; marrying positions; changing the story after a loss ("I'm now a long-term investor"); having no exit plan set in advance; ignoring the possibility that the world had changed; and treating a losing position's cheapness as an argument to buy more. Absence of doubt, the trait that made their run possible, made their ruin certain. The market's winners at any moment are often those most exquisitely adapted to the past — and most fragile to the future.
The Median Isn't the Message: Averages Lie Under Asymmetry

The paleontologist Stephen Jay Gould, diagnosed with abdominal mesothelioma, was told the disease had a median survival of eight months. Digging into the statistics, he realized the distribution was strongly right-skewed: half of patients died within eight months, but the surviving half stretched out over many years, and someone young, optimistic, and well-treated sat in the favorable region of that long tail. His essay title became a slogan for the whole chapter's argument — "the median isn't the message." Gould lived another twenty years.

The lesson generalizes: under asymmetry, no single summary number — median, mean, or mode — tells you what you need to know, and the "typical" outcome can be an outright lie about the expected one. Taleb's own book at the time carried the same structure. An asymmetric bet can leave you below water at the median — the most representative single scenario is a loss — while carrying a strongly positive expectation because the rare winning scenarios are enormous.

This reframes everyday statistical talk. "The market usually goes up," "this strategy is profitable in most years," "the average patient survives eight months" — all are frequency statements smuggled in as value statements. They answer how often, when the decision-relevant question is how much, weighted by how often. Whenever someone quotes you a central tendency, the first question is whether the distribution is symmetric. If it isn't, the average is at best incomplete and at worst an inversion of the truth — flattering doomed strategies (blowup traders' smooth averages) and slandering sound ones (bleed traders' ugly medians). Skewness is where intuition, summary statistics, and short track records all fail at once, and for the same reason.
In practice
Before any decision under uncertainty, write down both columns: probability of each outcome and its magnitude. Act on the product — the expectation — never on which outcome is merely most likely.

Audit your income, portfolio, and career for hidden short-the-rare-event exposure: any smooth, reliable payoff stream deserves the question "what rare event am I implicitly insuring, and can I survive it?"

Treat unusually consistent track records — a fund, a strategy, an advisor — as a red flag under asymmetry, not a credential; ask what the worst unobserved month could look like, not what the observed average was.

Prefer bleed to blowup where you can: structure bets with capped, known downside and open-ended upside (insurance, optionality, cheap experiments), and pre-commit to tolerating long stretches of small losses.
Write your exit plan before entering any position or project, and never average down or reframe a losing trade as a "long-term investment" after the fact.
When quoted a median or average — survival rates, returns, salaries — ask whether the distribution is skewed before drawing any conclusion; under asymmetry, demand the full shape, not the summary.
